title: Perhaps not Boring Technology after all
description: Simon Willison challenges the concern that LLMs for programming push technology choices toward tools best represented in training data. He finds that modern coding agents with long context windows can work effectively with new, private, or niche tools by consuming documentation and learning from existing code patterns. The Skills mechanism is emerging as a way for projects to provide official integrations for coding agents.
author: Simon Willison

title: OpenAI offers Codex for Open Source maintainers
description: OpenAI launched a comparable offer to Anthropic's: six months of ChatGPT Pro (same $200/month price as Claude Max) with Codex and 'conditional access to Codex Security' for core maintainers. Unlike Anthropic they don't specify exact metrics but the application asks for GitHub stars, monthly downloads, or why the project is important to the ecosystem.
author: Simon Willison

title: Agentic manual testing - coding agents can execute code
description: Simon Willison explains that the defining characteristic of a coding agent is that it can execute the code it writes. This makes coding agents much more useful than LLMs that simply output code without verification. Never assume code generated by an LLM works until that code has been executed. Coding agents can confirm their code works as intended, or iterate further until it does.
author: Simon Willison

title: Clinejection - Compromising Cline's Production Releases via Prompt Injection
description: Adnan Khan describes a sophisticated attack chain against the Cline GitHub repository starting with prompt injection in an issue title. The attack involved tricking Claude Code (used for issue triage) into installing malicious packages via npm install, then exploiting GitHub Actions cache eviction to poison caches shared between workflows, ultimately stealing NPM publishing secrets. Cline failed to handle the bug report promptly and were exploited - version 2.3.0 was published by an attacker.
author: Simon Willison (covering Adnan Khan)

title: Anthropic and the Pentagon - AI governance analysis
description: Bruce Schneier and Nathan E. Sanders provide thoughtful coverage of the Pentagon/OpenAI/Anthropic contract situation. They note that AI models are increasingly commodified with similar performance across top providers, making branding crucial. Anthropic is positioning itself as the moral and trustworthy AI provider, which has market value for both consumers and enterprise clients.
author: Simon Willison (covering Bruce Schneier and Nathan E. Sanders)

title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.4 with 1M Token Context Window
description: OpenAI announced GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4-pro with 1 million token context window and August 31, 2025 knowledge cutoff. GPT-5.4 beats the coding specialist GPT-5.3-Codex on all relevant benchmarks. Notable focus on business applications with 87.3% score on spreadsheet modeling tasks versus 68.4% for GPT-5.2. The model includes image generation capabilities.
author: OpenAI (covered by Simon Willison)

title: 2026 AI investments will cost more than the Moon landing
description: Major tech companies (Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet) are planning to spend $670 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026. This investment exceeds the cost of the Moon landing program, highlighting the massive scale of AI capital expenditure.
author: The Verge

title: Organizational Context for AI Coding Agents
description: Software Engineering Daily podcast episode discussing AI coding agents with Dennis Pilarinos. Covers organizational considerations for deploying AI coding agents in production environments.
author: Software Engineering Daily

title: SED News: OpenClaw Goes Viral, Mistral's Compute Play, and the Agent Arms Race
description: Software Engineering Daily news episode covering OpenClaw going viral, Mistral's computing strategy, and developments in AI agents competition.
author: Software Engineering Daily

title: Something is afoot in the land of Qwen
description: Simon Willison reports on major departures from Alibaba's Qwen team, including lead researcher Junyang Lin and several core members. This comes as the team released Qwen 3.5, an exceptional family of open weight models ranging from 397B to 0.8B parameters. The 2B model is particularly notable at just 4.57GB with full reasoning and multi-modal capabilities.
author: Simon Willison

title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite - Google's latest inexpensive model
description: Google released Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, an update to their inexpensive Flash-Lite family. At $0.25/million tokens of input and $1.5/million output this is 1/8th the price of Gemini 3.1 Pro. It supports four different thinking levels for varying reasoning depth.
author: Simon Willison

title: AI Developer Tools Efficiency Trap
description: Analysis of why developers are working longer hours despite AI code generation making things faster. Discusses the 'efficiency trap' phenomenon, compares usage differences between developers in China and the US, and explores how to avoid the 'generate-review-debug' vicious cycle.
author: AI Research Community

title: GitHub: Humans Can't Compete with AI Anymore
description: According to SemiAnalysis, Anthropic's Claude Code now contributes 4% of public GitHub commits, projected to reach 20% of daily commits by end of 2026. Highlights the growing dominance of AI in code contributions.
author: SemiAnalysis (covered by GeekPark)

title: chardet v7.0.0 AI-Assisted Code Controversy
description: Hacker News discussion about the open-source project 'chardet' v7.0.0 controversy. The project used AI-assisted code rewriting which raised copyright and licensing disputes, as it was originally based on Mozilla's LGPL license.
author: Hacker News Community

title: IronEngine: Towards General AI Assistant
description: arXiv paper presenting system design, multi-model orchestration, and engineering practices for building general AI assistants.
author: Researchers

title: Human-Certified Module Repositories for the AI Age
description: arXiv paper introducing Human-Certified Module Repositories (HCMRs) for trustworthy software construction in the era of AI-generated code.
author: Researchers

title: Causality Elicitation from Large Language Models
description: arXiv paper exploring techniques for extracting causal relationships from large language models.
author: Researchers

title: Deep Research for Recommender Systems
description: arXiv paper arguing for moving beyond traditional 'tool-based' paradigms in recommender systems toward deeper research approaches.
author: Researchers
